questions.prompt: Listen to the voicemail. What time does Wednesday's choir practice now start?
questions.passage: TRANSCRIPT: [Woman] Hi, it's Priya from the choir. Just a quick note about Wednesday. We were going to begin at six o'clock, but the hall is being used for an exam until then, so we'll start at seven instead. We should still finish by half past eight. See you there!

questions.prompt: Listen to the announcement. Where should new members go?
questions.passage: TRANSCRIPT: [Man] Good evening, everyone, and welcome to our open evening. The café is here on the ground floor, and the concert hall is straight ahead of you. New members, though, should go up to Room 4 on the first floor, where the registration desk is. We begin in ten minutes.

questions.prompt: Listen to the conversation in a shop. What does the woman buy?
questions.passage: TRANSCRIPT: [Woman] Excuse me, do you sell violin strings? [Man] I'm sorry, we've run out of violin strings today. We've got guitar strings and drumsticks. [Woman] Oh dear. Well, my brother needs drumsticks anyway, so I'll take those, please. [Man] Certainly. That's four pounds.

questions.prompt: Listen to the message. Which day has the piano lesson moved to?
questions.passage: TRANSCRIPT: [Woman] Hello, this is a message about your piano lesson. Your usual Monday lesson can't go ahead this week, because the teacher is away. We thought about Tuesday, but the studio is already booked then, so your lesson will be on Thursday, at the same time as always.

questions.prompt: Listen to the voicemail. Why has Saturday's concert been postponed?
questions.passage: TRANSCRIPT: [Man] Hi Yusuf, it's about Saturday's concert. It's not the weather, luckily, and the singers are all fine. The problem is the sound system — the speakers were damaged when the roof leaked last week, and the replacements won't arrive until Monday. So we've had to move the concert to the following Saturday.

choices: One of the singers is ill

questions.prompt: Listen to the announcement. How much does a student pay for a ticket?
questions.passage: TRANSCRIPT: [Woman] Attention, please. Tickets for the summer showcase are now available. The full price is eighteen pounds, and club members pay twelve. Students, however, pay only ten. A family ticket for four people costs thirty-five. Please book at the front desk before Friday.

questions.prompt: Listen to the conversation. What will the two speakers do first?
questions.passage: TRANSCRIPT: [Man] Shall we go straight into the rehearsal room? [Woman] The others won't be here for another half an hour. [Man] We could set up the microphones now, then. [Woman] Actually, the caretaker still has the key to the equipment cupboard, and he's at lunch. Let's grab a coffee downstairs and come back at four. [Man] Fine by me.

questions.prompt: Listen to the message. How does the speaker suggest travelling to the festival?
questions.passage: TRANSCRIPT: [Woman] Hi, it's about Sunday's music festival down at the old harbour. Don't take the car — the car park closed for building work last month. The riverboat is lovely, but it only runs twice a day. To be honest, the tram is your best bet: it stops right at the gate and runs every twelve minutes.

questions.prompt: Listen to the announcement. What is happening at three o'clock?
questions.passage: TRANSCRIPT: [Man] Welcome to the college music day. Here's what's on. At one, there's a guitar workshop in Studio B. At two, our visiting composer talks about writing music for films. At three, the student orchestra performs in the main hall, and at four we finish with an open singing session for everybody.

choices: a performance by the student orchestra

questions.prompt: Listen to the voicemail. What does the caller want the listener to do?
questions.passage: TRANSCRIPT: [Woman] Hi Ellis, it's Maya from the committee. The programmes have all been printed, so that's done — thanks for sorting that out. The thing is, two more players have joined the string group since we sent the list off, so the names on stage won't match the programme. Could you prepare a small paper insert with the extra names before Friday?

choices: prepare an insert with the new names
find two more players for the orchestra

questions.prompt: Listen to the conversation about a music practice app. How does the woman feel about it?
questions.passage: TRANSCRIPT: [Man] Have you tried that new practice app everyone's talking about? [Woman] I downloaded it last week. The lessons are beautifully made, I'll say that for it. [Man] So you'll carry on with it, then? [Woman] I'm honestly not sure. It keeps interrupting me to sell me things, and for what they charge every month, I did expect it to work without an internet connection. [Man] Fair enough.

choices: She is delighted and will definitely keep it
She thinks it is excellent value for money
She has mixed feelings and has not decided

questions.prompt: Listen to the announcement. What should ticket holders for Hall B do?
questions.passage: TRANSCRIPT: [Woman] May I have your attention, please. This evening's recital in Hall B has been moved because of a problem with the heating. Ticket holders for Hall B should go instead to the Riverside Theatre, which is five minutes' walk along the path behind this building. Your tickets remain valid and the start time is unchanged.

questions.prompt: Listen to the conversation. Why has the group decided to record at home?
questions.passage: TRANSCRIPT: [Man] Are you still booking the studio for the album? [Woman] We've decided to record at home instead. [Man] Is that because the studio is too expensive? [Woman] Partly, but mainly because our drummer is looking after her father this month and can't travel across the city. Recording at home means she can join us in the evenings. [Man] That makes sense.

choices: They prefer the sound at home
One member is unable to travel

questions.prompt: Four people talk about why they joined a music group. Match each statement to the correct speaker.
questions.passage:
TRANSCRIPT: Speaker A: I only started singing because of my grandfather. He sang in a choir for forty years, and when he became too ill to go, I promised him I would take his place. I'm certainly not the best voice in the room, but every Tuesday I feel that he's there with me.
Speaker B: I sit in front of a screen writing code all day, and by six o'clock my head is completely full. Playing the double bass is the only thing that empties it. I don't practise to get better any more — I practise to stop thinking.
Speaker C: My reason is embarrassingly practical, I'm afraid. Universities abroad look at what you do outside the classroom, and an orchestra looks good on the form. I fully expected to hate it. Two years on, I'm still here, and the application was sent long ago.
Speaker D: I've been playing the flute since I was seven, but always alone in my room. What I really wanted was people. Now I know thirty names, we go out for noodles after every rehearsal, and half of them have become proper friends.

statements: This person began for a reason connected with a university application.
This person wanted company instead of playing alone.
This person joined in order to keep a promise to a family member.
This person uses music to clear their mind after work.

questions.prompt: Listen to two people discussing whether music lessons should be compulsory in schools. Who expresses each opinion?
questions.passage: TRANSCRIPT: [Woman] Did you read that the education board wants music to be compulsory for every pupil until sixteen? [Man] I saw that, and I'm all for it. Music teaches you patience and teamwork in a way that very few other subjects do. [Woman] I'd agree with the teamwork part, actually. Playing in a group taught me more about listening to other people than any lesson ever did. [Man] Exactly my point. [Woman] My worry is where the time is supposed to come from. Something has to be dropped, and it's usually whichever subject the parents complain about least. [Man] That's fair, although I'd argue the timetable isn't nearly as full as head teachers pretend. [Woman] And instruments cost money. A school in a poorer area simply can't buy thirty violins. [Man] It doesn't have to. Singing costs nothing at all, and several of the countries with the finest music education begin with the voice and nothing else. [Woman] Hmm. I hadn't thought of that. [Man] What does worry me is forcing it on teenagers who genuinely hate it. Compulsory anything can kill the enjoyment. [Woman] Oh, absolutely. Make it a requirement and by fifteen half of them will never touch an instrument again.

statements: Schools with less money may be unable to buy instruments.
Music develops the ability to work as part of a team.
Forcing teenagers to study music could destroy their enjoyment of it.
Music can be taught cheaply through singing.

questions.prompt: Listen to the extract from a talk about tunes that stay in our heads, and answer both questions.
questions.passage: TRANSCRIPT: [Female speaker] Almost everyone has had a tune lodge itself in their head and refuse to leave. Psychologists call these involuntary musical images, though most of us prefer the blunter word: earworms. The obvious explanation — that catchy songs are simply well written — turns out to be only a small part of the story. The tunes that stick tend to be fast, simple in shape, and yet they contain one small surprise: an unexpected leap, or an odd repetition that the brain cannot quite file away. In other words, an earworm is not a perfect melody but a slightly unfinished one. The mind, faced with a pattern it cannot complete, keeps replaying it in the hope of closing the loop. That would explain why the cure most people stumble on by accident actually works: listening to the whole song, right through to the final note, tends to release you, while trying hard not to think about the tune does precisely the opposite. What interests me most, though, is how neatly this fits a much broader principle. Memory, it seems, is not drawn to the smooth and the finished. It clings to the things that are almost, but not quite, resolved.

questions.text: What is the speaker's main point about earworms?
choices: They are simply the result of well-written, catchy songs
They persist because the brain cannot complete an unresolved pattern
They affect some listeners far more than others
They are caused by hearing the same song too many times

questions.text: What does the speaker suggest about trying hard to forget an earworm?
choices: It usually works within a few minutes
It is the method most often recommended by psychologists
It tends to make the problem worse
It works only if the song is unfamiliar

questions.prompt: Listen to the extract from a talk about music recommendation services, and answer both questions.
questions.passage: TRANSCRIPT: [Male speaker] Ask most people how they discover new music now, and they will point at an algorithm. The recommendation engine has become the friend who never runs out of suggestions, and I use it as much as anyone. But I want to put a slightly awkward question. What exactly is a recommendation engine optimising for? Not, I would suggest, your musical growth. It is optimising for the next click — for the track you are least likely to skip. And the safest way to stop you skipping is to hand you something reassuringly close to what you already like. The result is a strange kind of comfort. Your listening becomes smoother, more agreeable, and quietly narrower. The discoveries that once changed people — the record a cousin pushed into your hands, the band you sat through simply because you had already paid — were uncomfortable precisely because nobody had calculated that you would enjoy them. Now, I am not calling for anyone to switch these services off; that ship has sailed. I am suggesting something smaller and rather harder: that once a month you choose something deliberately unlike yourself, sit through all of it, and see what happens.

questions.text: What is the speaker's central criticism of recommendation algorithms?
choices: They are technically unreliable and often fail
They suggest music that is too difficult for most listeners
They keep listeners comfortable and gradually narrow their taste
They pay musicians too little for each play

questions.text: What does the speaker's tone suggest about his own use of these services?
choices: He has stopped using them completely
He uses them himself and does not expect people to abandon them
He has never trusted them at all
He believes they will soon disappear
